intitule: Lesson 21 Assessment: Paired Conjunctions (Either...Or / Neither...Nor / Not Only...But Also)

enonce.texte: Read each sentence carefully. Choose the correct option to complete the gap, respecting the rules of subject-verb agreement and parallel structure.

questions.texte: ______ the Principal ______ the Bursar is available right now; both are in a meeting about the new scholarship program.

sousQuestions.texte: Justify your choice by explaining the rule used.
sousQuestions.reponse: The correct answer is 'Neither...nor' because the sentence indicates that both people are unavailable (combined negation), and the singular verb 'is' follows the rule that with 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the subject closest to it ('the Bursar' = singular).

questions.texte: You can pay for your online course ______ by credit card ______ by mobile money (MTN or Orange). Both methods are accepted.

sousQuestions.texte: Rewrite the sentence using 'or' only, without changing the meaning.
sousQuestions.reponse: You can pay for your online course by credit card or by mobile money (MTN or Orange).

questions.texte: ______ did the student finish his civic education project on time, ______ he helped three classmates with theirs.

sousQuestions.texte: Transform the sentence into a standard (non-inverted) sentence.
sousQuestions.reponse: The student not only finished his civic education project on time but also helped three classmates with theirs.

questions.texte: For the position of polling station supervisor, you need ______ a degree in public law ______ previous experience with ELECAM. One of the two is sufficient.
______ the lack of transparency ______ the low voter turnout was discussed during the governance seminar.

sousQuestions.texte: Explain the inversion rule applied in this sentence.
sousQuestions.reponse: When 'Not only' begins a sentence for emphasis, it requires subject-verb inversion (auxiliary 'was' comes before the subject 'the lack'). The second part 'but also' introduces an additional element without inversion. This structure is used to add emphasis.

intitule: Real-Life Application: School Debate on Drug Prevention

enonce.texte: You are the president of the 'Anti-Drug Club' at your high school in Yaoundé. You are preparing a speech for a debate against another school. Complete your argument using either...or, neither...nor, and not only...but also.

questions.texte: Formulate a sentence to explain that drugs have TWO negative consequences: health problems AND academic failure. Use 'Not only...but also' with inversion for emphasis.
questions.reponse: Not only do drugs cause severe health issues, but they also lead to academic failure and school dropout.

questions.texte: Your opponent says students must choose between 'smoking cannabis' or 'drinking alcohol' to be cool. Refute this by stating that BOTH options are unacceptable for a responsible youth. Use 'Neither...nor'.
questions.reponse: Neither smoking cannabis nor drinking alcohol is acceptable for a responsible Cameroonian youth.

sousQuestions.texte: Explain why the verb 'is' (singular) is correct here.
sousQuestions.reponse: The verb is singular because with 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the subject closest to it. Here, 'drinking alcohol' is singular (gerund), so the verb 'is' is used.

intitule: Writing Task: Letter to the Editor about Juvenile Delinquency

enonce.texte: Context: You read an article claiming that delinquency in your neighborhood comes from either a lack of parental control or bad company. You disagree. Write two sentences for a letter to the editor (Le Jour newspaper) using the targeted structures.

questions.texte: Write one sentence using 'Neither...nor' to explain that the real causes are BOTH poverty AND the absence of recreational centers (not just family or friends).
questions.reponse: In my community, neither a lack of parental control nor bad company is the main cause of juvenile delinquency.

questions.texte: Write a second sentence using 'Not only...but also' to emphasize that poverty destroys opportunities AND pushes kids toward violence.
questions.reponse: Not only does poverty destroy educational and professional opportunities for our youth, but it also pushes them toward violence and petty crime.

sousQuestions.texte: Identify the auxiliary verb used after 'Not only' and explain why.
sousQuestions.reponse: The auxiliary verb 'does' is used because the main verb 'destroys' is in the present simple. Inversion with 'Not only' requires an auxiliary verb; since there is no modal or 'be', we add 'do/does'.
